coredata.dc:title: A magnetofluorescent boron-doped carbon dots as a metal-free bimodal probe
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Xiuxiu
Dong, Luxi
Ming, Yali
Wang, Miao
Lu, Zechao
Xu, Yang
Li, Huanrong
coredata.dc:description: High-resolution observation of biological process is vital for biological researches and diagnosing diseases, which requires accurate diagnosis that involves coordinating imaging technologies such as fluorescence and magnetic resonance (MR). Nowadays, metal-based labels have been used for dual modality imaging. However, heavy metal ions are not environment-and organism-friendly. Therefore, it is a desirable to fabricate a metal-free label with fluorescence and MR properties. Herein, we synthesized boron–doped carbon dots (B–CDs) with dual modal properties through a one-pot solvothermal process. Compared with boron-free CDs, B–CDs exhibited apparent red-shift fluoresence emission, higher fluorescence intensity, and higher longitudinal relaxivity (r1 = 5.13 mM−1 s−1). It demonstrated that boron doping can enhance the fluorescence intensity of CDs, and maybe lead to form paramagnetic centers. The fluorescence and MR imaging of B–CDs make them a prospective label for clinical applications as a result of their oversimplified synthesis process, low cost, good biocompatibility and low toxicity. It will open a new window for building novel imaging labels.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Magnetic resonance
Boron

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cell viability test and in vitro imaging
In vitro and in vivo imaging

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image of B–CDs prepared from PTB via a solvothermal process. Inset: size distribution of B–CDs obtained from TEM images. (B) HRTEM image of B–CDs. Scan bar was 5 nm. Inset: HRTEM image of B–CDs with lattice spacing of 0.22 nm. (C) FT-IR spectra of TB, PTB, and B-CDs. (D) HR-XPS B 1s spectrum of B–CDs.

(A) UV/Vis absorbance spectra of TB and B–CDs. (B) The bright-field and (C) fluorescence photograph of (a) PTB and (b) B–CDs. (D) Fluorescence-emission and fluorescence-excitation spectra of B–CDs. (E) Fluorescence spectra and images of (a) CDs prepared from polythiophene, and (b) B–CDs.
The longitudinal (r1) relaxivity curve of B–CDs. Inset: In vitro MR images of B–CDs using different boron concentrations: 0, 13, 41, 81, and 135 μM.
Bright-field and fluorescence images of HeLa cells upon treatment with (B–CDs, 30 μg/mL) and without B–CDs (blank) for 2 h.
The in vivo fluorescence and MR (inset) imaging of nude mice (A) before and (B) after subcutaneous injection of B–CDs.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis of B–CDs.

head.ce:title: A magnetofluorescent boron-doped carbon dots as a metal-free bimodal probe

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Hebei University of Technology, Guangrong Dao No. 8, Hongqiao District, Tianjin 300130, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical Engineering and Technology, Hebei University of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Medicinal Chemical Biology, Nankai University, Tianjin 300071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Medicinal Chemical Biology, Nankai University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: High-resolution observation of biological process is vital for biological researches and diagnosing diseases, which requires accurate diagnosis that involves coordinating imaging technologies such as fluorescence and magnetic resonance (MR). Nowadays, metal-based labels have been used for dual modality imaging. However, heavy metal ions are not environment-and organism-friendly. Therefore, it is a desirable to fabricate a metal-free label with fluorescence and MR properties. Herein, we synthesized boron–doped carbon dots (B–CDs) with dual modal properties through a one-pot solvothermal process. Compared with boron-free CDs, B–CDs exhibited apparent red-shift fluoresence emission, higher fluorescence intensity, and higher longitudinal relaxivity (r1 = 5.13 mM−1 s−1). It demonstrated that boron doping can enhance the fluorescence intensity of CDs, and maybe lead to form paramagnetic centers. The fluorescence and MR imaging of B–CDs make them a prospective label for clinical applications as a result of their oversimplified synthesis process, low cost, good biocompatibility and low toxicity. It will open a new window for building novel imaging labels.

ce:section.ce:para: Observation of biological processes with high resolution has been widely used as a vital way to develop the biological researches and diagnose clinical diseases, which requires the labels for lightening lesion locations [1,2]. Thus, the ability of a label plays an important role for accurate diagnosis. However, the intractable problem of labels selection is that several kinds of labels for imaging such as fluorescence, magnetic, and radioactive labels, have their advantages and limitations. For example, fluorescence labels own the merit of high sensitivity, but are restricted by poor penetration of light. In contrast, magnetic labels offer high spatial resolution and tissue penetration but suffer from low sensitivity. Therefore, fluorescence/MR dual imaging is attractive because of its advantages integration and cross-validation.

To date, several Gd-based contrast agents as MR source have been used for dual modality imaging. Sun et al. reported a Gd2O3/Au hybrid nanomaterial for dual modality imaging of fluorescence and MR [3]. Mulder et al. designed CdSe/ZnS core-shell quantum dots covered with a Gd–DTPA based paramagnetic lipid [4]. Carbon dots (CDs) with exceptional optical properties and favorable biocompatibility [5–9], are also doped with Gd3+ to endow them with MR activity. Bourlinos et al. proposed a one-pot carbonization process to synthesize fluorescence/MR bimodal Gd–doped CDs [10]. Chen et al. prepared Gd–encapsulated CDs with r1 relaxivity of 5.88 mM−1 s−1 for in vivo imaging [11]. However, heavy metal ions, especially Gd ions are not friendly to environment and organism. Free Gd ions can inhibit calcium channels, cause cardiovascular and neurologic toxicity [12]. Gd ions leakage is an important index for assessing the ability of Gd-based contrast agents, and need several experiments to verify. Therefore, a novel metal-free probe as MR contrast agent is a desirable to simplify experiment process, avoid Gd leakage, and improve biocompatibility.

Herein, we report a metal-free boron doped CDs (B–CDs) nanomaterials via one-pot solvothermal treatment of polythiophene-3-boronic acid (PTB) at 170 °C. Notably, we found that B–CDs exhibited an r1 relaxivity of 5.13 mM−1 s−1 based on the concentration of boron. The MR activity is very close to previously reported Gd–doped CDs [11]. Moreover, the effect of boron doping was carefully investigated. B–CDs exhibited a 38 nm red-shift in fluorescence emission and 36 times intensity enhancement than boron-free CDs, meanwhile boron doping area maybe affect localized spins to form paramagnetic centers. Cell viability and histological changes test were performed to verify good biocompatibility of B–CDs. The dual modal signals of B–CDs were confirmed using nude mice as the model. Compared to other bimodal labels, these B–CDs did not consist of metal ions and exhibited low cost, good biocompatibility and low toxicity. It will open a new window for building novel imaging labels.

Ferric chloride (FeCl3) was purchased from Energy Chemical Co., China. The solvents, such as chloroform, methanol, and ethanol were purchased by Tianjin Fengchuan Chemical reagent technologies, China. Thiophene-3-boronic acid (TB) was acquired from J&K Scientific, China. All the reagents and chemicals were at analytical reagent, and were used without purification.

The morphology of the B–CDs were characterized with a Tecnai G2 F20 transmission electron microscope (TEM), which was working at a 200 kV accelerating voltage. An ultrathin carbon film based copper TEM substrate (Zhongjingkeyi Technology Co., Ltd., China) was used for TEM analysis. UV–vis absorption spectra of B–CDs and thiophene-3-boronic acid were measured by using an Agilent Carry 100 UV–vis spectrophotometer at room temperature. The fluorescence experiments for investigating fluorescence properties of B–CDs and Boron-free CDs were obtained with an FL-4600 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured on Thermo ESCALAB 250XI. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were performed using a Bruker Vector 22 spectrophotometer with KBr pellets.

PTB was prepared via an oxidative polymerization according to the previous report [13]. Briefly, 4 equivalents of FeCl3 were dispersed in 30 mL of anhydrous CHCl3 with the protection of nitrogen, stirring to ensure that FeCl3 was uniformly mixed. Finally, 1 equivalent of thiophene-3-boronic acid was dispersed in 20 mL of CHCl3 and sonicated for 5 min, followed by stirring at room temperature for 2 days. The precipitate was collected, followed by washing with methanol. Black PTB powder was finally obtained by drying under vacuum.

In a typical synthesis, B–CDs were prepared through a facile one-pot hydrothermal process. PTB (7.5 mg) was added in ethanol (10 mL), which was treated ultrasonically for 30 min and then transferred into 30 mL Teflon lined autoclave and heated at 170 °C for 12 h. After nature cooling to room temperature, the B–CDs were collected in order to remove large scale particles, through filtering using 0.22-μm membranes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell viability test and in vitro imaging
ce:section.ce:para: The cytotoxicity of B–CDs was evaluated on the HeLa cell line, by using an MTT assay. Generally, the cells were seeded in 96-well culture-medium at a density of 105 cells per well, followed by incubation for 12 h. After the culture-medium was substituted with fresh Dulbecco's modified Eagle medium (DMEM; 400 μL), B–CDs were added into the wells, mixing with medium to dilute the concentration (0–40 μg/mL). Then, the wells were added with MTT solution, and the cells were incubation for 4 h again. 150 μL of N,N′-Dimethyl sulfoxide was introduced to liberate the formazan crystals. The cell viability of B–CDs was evaluated by using a Molecular Devices Spectra Max i3 recording the absorbance at 490 nm. The cytotoxicity tests were performed three times under identical conditions. HeLa cells were seeded in 6-well culture-medium. After overnight incubation in water at 37 °C, the cells were treated with the solution of B–CDs (concentration: 30 μg/mL) for 2 h. The cells were then washed 3 times with phosphate buffer saline (PBS) and cell images were observed with a confocal laser fluorescence microscopy (TCS SP5, Leica, Germany). The cells treated with PBS, which did not contain B–CDs, were denoted as the control group for fluorescence imaging.

An Edinburgh FS920P fluorescence spectrometer equipped with an integrating sphere was used for testing the quantum yield (QY) measurements. The pre-treatment of samples were obtained according to the manual provided by Edinburgh Instrument. The samples added to a 5 mL cuvette were excited upon 430 nm mono-chromatic light excitation.
The fluorescence spectra were recorded in the ranges of 425–435 and 450–650 nm. The QYs were calculated by fluorescence software. The same processes were performed three times.

The animal experiments in this work were consistent with the guidelines of the Institutional Animal Care Committee of Nankai University. The T1-weighted magnetic resonance detection and imaging of B–CDs were performed with a 1.2 T MR imaging system (Huantong, Shanghai, China) equipped with a 50 mm animal coil and a fat-saturated 3D gradient echo imaging sequence. The fluorescence imaging experiment for testing the performance of B–CDs was carried out with a small animal in vivo imaging system (NightOWL LB983, Berthold Technologies GmbH & Co. KG, Germany) equipped with IndiGO sofeware. The fluorescence imaging was performed according to the fluorescence properties of B–CDs (Excitation wavelength: 480 nm; Emission wavelength: 535 nm).

An oxidative polymerization process was performed with thiophene-3-boronic acid (TB) polymerization according to previous report by using FeCl3 as catalysis [13]. Through the reaction, the powder color was changed from white to black, indicating that polymerization was successfully achieved (Fig. S1). The thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) exhibited that the weight change of PTB occur from 71 °C due to decomposition of functional groups (Fig. S2). B–CDs were prepared by a one-pot solvothermal reaction, as shown in Scheme 1. Polythiophenes-3-boronic acid (PTB) as both the carbon source and boron source was carbonized at 170 °C (Scheme 1). Boron–doped graphene is usually prepared from graphene oxide under high temperature [14], whereas this proposed method did not require complex procedure because of the presence of boron in PTB. It was only required to be suitable for carbonization. Notably, PTB could not be dissolved in ethanol or water, whereas the solution of as-prepared B–CDs was transparent. It illustrated that PTB was carbonized to B–CDs with good solubility.

The morphology of as-prepared B–CDs was obtained by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), as shown in Fig. 1. Fig. 1A–B shows that the size distribution of the as-prepared B–CDs were focused in the extent of 2–9 nm with the average diameter of 3.5 nm, as depicted in Inset of Fig. 1A. Lattice spacing of 0.22 nm in the B–CDs (Inset in Fig. 1B) was observed using high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM), which corresponded to the (100) facet of graphite [5], illustrating that PTB was successfully carbonized to form B–CDs. A peak at 23 degree can be observed in XRD patterns of B–CDs (Fig. S3), which is similar to previously reported CDs [13]. No well-defined diffraction peaks can be observed because of amorphous structure of B–CDs.

Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of B–CDs, PTB, and thiophene-3-boronic acid (TB) were exhibited in Fig. 1C. B–CDs, PTB, and TB had three similar peaks at 1023 cm−1, 1098 cm−1, and 1634 cm−1, which were associated to the stretch of B–OH, B–C, and C=C, respectively [15]. It demonstrated that boron still remained in B–CDs through the solvothermal process. The peaks at 686 cm-1 and 786 cm-1 were related to the vibration bond of C-S in thiophene ring and C-H out-of-plane deformation mode [16], but this peak cannot be observed in B-CDs sample. It demonstrated that thiophene was carbonized. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was further performed to investigate the chemical composition of B–CDs (Fig. 1D). Boron was observed at 190.6 eV, with a content of 0.77% in XPS analysis (Table S1). The B1s band could be deconvoluted into two peaks. The peak at 188.8 eV could be ascribed to B-sub-C bond, indicating boron was influenced the constitution of thiophene ring and successfully doped in carbon dots [17,18]. The peak at 192.6 eV was attributed to B–O bonding structure [19]. No Fe and Cl ions were observed in the data obtained from XPS analysis (Fig. S4). It demonstrated that the catalyst FeCl3 used in the synthesis of PTB was removed completely. Although the boron content in B–CDs was less than that in TB (7.18%, detected by XPS), it verified the existence of boron in as-prepared sample, which was corresponded to the results of FT-IR.

To investigate the optical properties of the as-prepared B–CDs, the B–CDs were further explored by UV/Vis absorption and fluorescence spectrum, as depicted in Fig. 2. In the UV/Vis spectra, the peaks were focused at 233 nm and 277 nm in an alcohol solution of TB (Fig. 2A). Absorption bands at 230 nm and 410 nm were observed in the B–CDs. It suggested that n-π* transition arises during carbonization process. PTB had low solubility and weak fluorescence (Fig. 2B–C). Notably, we found that the homogeneous solution of B-CDs exhibited bright green fluorescence (Fig. 2B–C). It inferred that alcohol participates in the form process and the solubility of B–CDs was improved. The fluorescence and fluorescence-excitation spectra of B–CDs were also tested (Fig. 2D). B–CDs showed a main excitation peak at 430 nm upon the detection emission wavelength of 535 nm. The emission spectrum exhibited that a maximum peak at 535 nm was observed.

The fluorescence quantum yield of B–CDs was calculated to be 58.7%. Further, no apparent decrease in the fluorescence emission could be observed after the treatment of 1 h of continuous excitation at 430 nm (Fig. S5). Boron-free CDs were prepared via same solvothermal treatment of polythiophene, which was in absence of boron. CDs exhibited a weak blue fluorescence at 497 nm (Fig. 2E). Thus, these results demonstrated that the fluorescence properties of CDs involving intensity and emission wavelength can be affected by the introduction of boron atom or boric acid group. Choi et al. prepared boron- and nitrogen-codoped CDs [20]. They found that these co-doped CDs had a higher fluorescence than nitrogen-doped CDs because the configuration of carbon and nitrogen in CDs was changed by doping of boron, and generated surface defect states [20], which was similar to these B–CDs. The optical results demonstrated that B–CDs had good optical property, and will be a potential probe for further optical applications.

To explore paramagnetic behaviour of the prepared B–CDs, magnetic resonance (MR) property was obtained using a 1.2 T MR scanner. Fig. 3 demonstrated that the T1 image intensity gradually enhanced with increase in concentration of boron. It indicated that contrast in T1-weighted MR could be strengthened by B–CDs. As shown in Fig. 3, the r1 relaxivity was calculated to be 5.13 mM−1 s−1. The r1 relaxivity of B–CDs was higher than that of Gd–DTPA (3.95 mM−1 s−1), which was a commercial MRI contrast agent. To investigate the effects of boron doping, the r1 relaxivity of boric acid, boric acid-glucose, and CDs prepared without boron was also tested [21]. No apparent r1 relaxivity signals could be observed in these samples (Fig. S6), and T1-weighted images also exhibited that B–CDs have a brighter MR signal than boron-free CDs (Inset of Fig. S6) It demonstrated that free boron in boric acid and B-O-C in boric acid-glucose complex have no contribution to MR signals. It is possible that boron combined with carbon atom affect the localized spins to generate the paramagnetic centers by doping with carbon core, which was similar to the previously reports [22–24].

In IR and XPS results, C–B–OH group is the dominant boron configuration in the B–CDs, which generates surface defect states (Fig. 1C–D). Approximately 38 nm red-shifts could be observed, compared with the boron-free CDs (Fig. 2E). Meanwhile, the combination between carbon and boron disturbs the structure of thiophene ring, and provides the isolated spins. Thus, surface defect states and paramagnetic centers were produced during boron doping process, which are mainly two reasons for B–CDs with fluorescence and MR properties [24]. To date, some fluorescent B–CDs have been reported for glucose detection [14,15,25]. It seems that some new fluorescence/MR bimodal labels will be produced in future.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro and in vivo imaging
ce:section.ce:para: In order to develop the bio-applications of the B–CDs, the cell viability treated with B–CDs was first investigated. The viability of HeLa cells after incubation with B–CDs was assessed by using the standard MTT assay. As shown in Fig. S7, 80 ± 1.17% of cell viability could be observed with 40 μg mL−1 of B–CDs. It demonstrated that B–CDs exhibit low toxicity to HeLa cells. Moreover, histological changes of organs (heart, liver, spleen, kidney, and intestine) was performed to assess the toxicity of B–CDs (Fig. S8). After 1 week post subcutaneous injection, there were no difference between control and experimental groups, demonstrating that no tissue damage form B–CDs.

To verify the feasibility of B–CDs for imaging, HeLa cells were incubated with B–CDs (30 μg mL−1) for cell imaging by a confocal laser scanning microscopy. After 2 h incubation, the cells treated with B–CDs became brightly green emission upon the 458 nm excitation, while no fluorescence signals could be observed in the control group under same condition ( Fig. 4). The fluorescence imaging results confirmed that the fluorescent B–CDs would be a potential label for cell imaging, and would be an alternative to metal-based labels or Gd-doped CDs because of their low cost, and environmentally- and organism-friendly.

Further, the B–CDs were used for in vivo imaging to illustrate the potentials for fluorescence and MR imaging because of their high luminescence and relaxation rates. Nude mice were injected with 200 μL of B–CDs solution. An apparent fluorescence signal could be observed after subcutaneous injection ( Fig. 5). Meanwhile, the MR images of the mice after subcutaneous injection exhibited brighter MR enhancement than the control (Inset of Fig. 5). Both fluorescence and MR results illustrated that the metal-free B-CDs are a potential probe for dual-modal imaging. To date, various B–CDs or boron-doped graphene quantum dots have been proposed (Table S2), but most of B–CDs are focus on fluorescence imaging of cell or fluorescence sensing of glucose. Therefore, these B–CDs with fluorescence and MR properties can become a potential alternative to Gd-based contrast agents for dual modality imaging because of their simple preparation, high contrast efficiency, and good biocompatibility.

We developed a facile route to synthesize nano-sized boron-carbon dots (B–CDs) with green fluorescence emission (QYs were calculated to be 58.7%), and good MR property with a longitudinal relaxivity of 5.13 mM−1 s−1. B–CDs exhibited a 38 nm red-shift in fluorescence emission and 36 times intensity enhancement than boron-free CDs, meanwhile boron doping area maybe affect localized spins to form paramagnetic centers. Compared with traditional metal-based MR contrast, these B–CDs did not consist of metal ions and had low cost, good biocompatibility and low toxicity. Therefore, B–CDs will be promising as a novel rising star for the biological applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial support by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21605036, 21771050), the Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (Nos. B2017202068, B2017202048) and the Educational Committee of Hebei Province (No. QN2016172) is acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2019.03.022.

Synthesis-modification integration: one-step fabrication of boronic acid functionalized carbon dots for fluorescent blood sugar sensing
Synthesis and characterization of conducting polythiophene/carbon nanotubes composites
